trajectory_summary: The call opens with a warm but avoidant customer on a recycled lead who has already seen a prior evaluation and was unimpressed. Brandon holds the appointment through persistence and correct scheduling pivots, but never surfaces what was wrong with the prior evaluation — leaving the closer to walk into an unresolved objection. The booking is real but fragile: the prior-evaluation disappointment is live, the bill has not been collected, and the confirmed time has a minor ambiguity that the closer should verify.
outcome_dispute: The call ends with a booked appointment (next Wednesday, nominally 3PM), so a 'booked' tag is defensible. However, the sit is fragile: Jared's prior-evaluation disappointment was never addressed, the bill was not collected on the call (Jared volunteered to send it separately), and Brandon's closing exchange introduces a possible verbal stumble — '3PM. Next Wednesday, I'll do 2PM. Right now. Yep.' at 02:50 — where the confirmed time is ambiguous. More likely a verbal stumble than a genuine competing proposal, but the closer should confirm the time before the sit.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Prior-evaluation objection — surface and address, don't skip
primary_coaching_focus.quote: I wasn't all that impressed right now. Let me just let me just circle off on on having you reach back out to me
primary_coaching_focus.why: Jared hands Brandon the exact reason he has been delaying for months, and Brandon moves past it without asking a single follow-up question. On a recycled lead, the prior evaluation is the objection — not logistics, not timing. Brandon's 'new solar program' framing is the right instinct but risks reading as a dismissal of Jared's concern rather than a genuine response to it. The closer now walks into an appointment where Jared's baseline is 'I wasn't all that impressed' with no context on what that means. Coach Brandon to make the implicit reframe explicit and to ask one diagnostic question: 'What was it about that evaluation that didn't land for you?' That answer is the closer's most valuable pre-call intelligence.

key_moments.quote: I wasn't all that impressed right now. Let me just let me just circle off on on having you reach back out to me, and and, I'll email you and and we'll set up a good time
key_moments.stewart_read: This is a recycled-lead prior-experience objection, not a changed-mind withdrawal. Jared is telling Brandon exactly why he has been delaying: the prior evaluation didn't land. Brandon's response at 00:55 implicitly reframes by calling this a 'new solar program' — directionally correct per rebuttals.already_saw_design — but he never asks what was unimpressive about the prior evaluation. Strategic read: Brandon made the right implicit move; he just didn't make it explicit. Reactive read: Brandon moved past the objection without resolving it. The strategic read is more supported — Brandon did label it 'new' — but the move needed one more sentence to land.

key_moments.quote: And what we talked about was showing you our new solar program. Mhmm. You don't need to pay anything upfront. We just wanna help you get a lower energy bill. So you just save money up every month.
key_moments.stewart_read: Brandon mentions 'lower energy bill' but does not anchor to Jared's actual bill amount or flip the bill into emotional alignment-against-the-utility. This is the bill-capture-and-drop failure pattern the schema identifies as universal across the corpus. On a recycled lead where Jared has already seen a prior evaluation, the bill anchor is the one lever that could differentiate this conversation — 'what are you paying now vs what you could be paying' — but Brandon doesn't pull it.

key_moments.quote: And with the programs that are still available, they help you switch over to solar at no cost to you.
key_moments.stewart_read: Brandon is pitching program features — 'no cost to you,' 'save money every month,' 'new program' — rather than creating curiosity about Jared's specific situation. This is scope creep into closer-tier content: the setter's job is to schedule, not to sell the program. The feature pitch also risks creating objections (Jared could ask 'what does no cost mean exactly?') that Brandon is not equipped to answer. The schema's curiosity_over_features principle is the corrective: make Jared curious to see his specific math, not sold on a generic program.

key_moments.quote: I can have a special just meet with you if you're available this Saturday.
key_moments.stewart_read: Brandon pushes Saturday before Jared has agreed to any time — and Jared had already signaled 'next week' at 00:32. This is a listening gap: Brandon is running a scheduling script rather than following Jared's stated availability. The Saturday push gets a flat 'No, man' rejection and costs Brandon a beat of rapport. The correct move at this point was to ask 'What does next week look like for you?' and let Jared name the window.
